status.state_description: Waiting for human approval of the plan
status.product_description: Headless ecommerce storefront with React frontend, Shopify/Medusa backend, product search (Algolia), cart management, checkout, and SSR for SEO.

plan.description: Analyze headless ecommerce market: competitor storefronts (Shopify Hydrogen, Commerce.js, BigCommerce), positioning for headless vs monolithic, target buyer personas, and GTM strategy.
acceptance_criteria: Competitor feature matrix covering search, cart, checkout, SSR capabilities
Documented buyer personas (2-3) with jobs-to-be-done
GTM strategy with channel recommendations and launch timeline
Positioning statement distinguishing headless_storefront value prop

plan.description: Build financial model covering infrastructure costs (Vercel/CDN, Algolia, Medusa hosting), Shopify API tier costs, projected revenue scenarios, and pricing strategy for the storefront platform.
acceptance_criteria: Monthly infrastructure cost estimate for 3 traffic tiers (1k, 10k, 100k sessions/month)
Break-even analysis at each tier
Shopify API plan comparison with cost impact documented
Algolia record/operation costs modeled against expected catalog size

plan.description: Conduct user research and produce wireframes for: product listing page (PLP), product detail page (PDP), search results with Algolia InstantSearch, cart drawer, multi-step checkout flow, and mobile-first responsive layout.
acceptance_criteria: Low-fidelity wireframes for all 8 pages reviewed by stakeholder
Mobile and desktop breakpoints documented for each page
Accessibility audit checklist completed — color contrast, keyboard nav, ARIA roles
Checkout flow UX validated against 3 usability test participants
Component inventory produced (button variants, form fields, product card)

plan.description: Draft terms of service, privacy policy (GDPR/CCPA), cookie consent policy, and IP/licensing review for third-party dependencies (Algolia SDK, Medusa, Next.js).
acceptance_criteria: Privacy policy covers all data collected (session, payment tokenization, search queries)
Cookie consent banner implementation spec included
Dependency license audit completed — no GPL conflicts in production bundle
PCI DSS scope reduction documented: cardholder data handled by Shopify/Stripe, not stored locally
Return/refund policy drafted
Consumer protection compliance checked

plan.description: Perform threat model for the headless storefront: API key exposure, XSS via product content, CSRF on cart mutations, Algolia API key scoping, PCI DSS SAQ-A scope definition, and dependency SBOM.
acceptance_criteria: STRIDE threat model completed for all API surfaces
Algolia search-only API key restrictions documented and verified in config
PCI DSS SAQ-A self-assessment questionnaire completed with all applicable items passing
SBOM generated listing all production dependencies with CVE scan results
CSP headers, HSTS, and X-Frame-Options defined in security policy doc
Payment flow PCI compliant
Fraud detection rules defined

plan.description: Define product requirements document (PRD): feature list, MVP scope, success metrics (Core Web Vitals, conversion rate, cart abandonment), and phased roadmap.
acceptance_criteria: PRD defines in-scope and out-of-scope features for v1
Success metrics measurable and baselined
Phased roadmap with v1 (MVP), v1.5 (personalization), v2 (loyalty) defined
Dependency on Shopify vs Medusa backend documented as decision with tradeoffs

plan.description: Design the API contract layer: Storefront API abstraction (Shopify Storefront GraphQL vs Medusa REST), cart service API, search API wrapper for Algolia, and OpenAPI/GraphQL schema for frontend consumption.
acceptance_criteria: GraphQL schema covers product, variant, collection, cart, checkout types
Medusa REST endpoints mapped for catalog, cart, order flows
Algolia index schema defined: product attributes, facets, ranking rules
Error response contracts standardized across all services
API versioning strategy documented

plan.description: Initialize monorepo structure: Next.js 14 App Router frontend, Medusa backend service, shared TypeScript types package, environment variable schema (.env.example), and ESLint/Prettier/TypeScript configs.

stack.backend: Medusa v2 (Node.js) or Shopify Storefront API

acceptance_criteria: pnpm workspace with packages: apps/web, apps/backend (if Medusa), packages/types
TypeScript strict mode enabled across all packages
Environment variables typed via zod schema validation at startup
Husky pre-commit hooks: lint + type-check passing
README with local dev setup instructions verified by running from scratch

plan.description: Set up Medusa database schema: products, variants, collections, customers, carts, orders tables. Configure PostgreSQL with migrations. If using Shopify only, document data model and skip migration setup.
acceptance_criteria: All Medusa v2 core entity migrations run successfully on clean PostgreSQL instance
Seed script populates 50+ test products across 5 collections
ERD diagram generated and reviewed
Database indexes defined on frequently queried fields (product handle, variant sku)
Connection pooling configured for production load

plan.description: Implement Medusa v2 backend: product catalog APIs, cart service (create, add item, update quantity, remove, apply discount), customer auth (register, login, JWT), and order creation endpoint.
acceptance_criteria: GET /products returns paginated list with variants and images
POST /carts creates cart and returns cart_id
POST /carts/:id/line-items adds product variant to cart
PATCH /carts/:id/line-items/:lid updates quantity, returns updated cart totals
POST /customers/auth returns signed JWT with 1h expiry and refresh token
All endpoints return consistent JSON error shape {code, message, details}
Rate limiter returns 429 after threshold with Retry-After header

plan.description: Implement checkout flow: address collection, shipping options, payment intent creation (Stripe integration via Medusa payment plugin), order confirmation, and webhook handler for payment events.
acceptance_criteria: POST /carts/:id/shipping-methods selects shipping option
POST /payment-sessions creates Stripe PaymentIntent and returns client_secret
POST /carts/:id/complete creates order on successful payment
Stripe webhook signature verified using STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET
Order confirmation email triggered on payment_intent.succeeded
Failed payment returns 402 with retry guidance

plan.description: Set up Algolia index sync: index products from Medusa/Shopify to Algolia on create/update/delete events using Medusa subscriber hooks. Define index settings: searchable attributes, facets, custom ranking.
acceptance_criteria: Product create/update in Medusa triggers Algolia index update within 5 seconds
Product delete removes record from Algolia index
Bulk reindex script processes 10,000 products without timeout
Algolia index settings script idempotent — safe to re-run
Search-only API key restricted to GET operations on products index only

plan.description: Implement Next.js 14 App Router foundation: layout, navigation, ThemeProvider, Cart context (Zustand), font optimization, and root error boundary. Implement SSR data fetching pattern with React Server Components.
acceptance_criteria: Root layout renders with correct HTML lang, meta charset, viewport
Cart context initializes from localStorage on hydration without flicker
Navigation responsive: hamburger menu on mobile, horizontal links on desktop
Error boundary catches and displays user-friendly error UI
React Server Component page renders with zero client JS for static content

plan.description: Build product listing page (PLP) and product detail page (PDP): SSR via generateStaticParams + ISR, product card component, image optimization (next/image), variant selector, add-to-cart action.
acceptance_criteria: PLP renders 24 products per page with pagination
PDP generateStaticParams pre-renders top 200 products at build time
Product images lazy-loaded with next/image blur placeholder
Variant selector updates price and availability without page reload
Add-to-cart updates cart count in header within 200ms
LCP on PDP < 2.5s measured in Lighthouse

plan.description: Integrate Algolia InstantSearch: search bar with autocomplete (SearchBox), search results page with faceted filtering (RefinementList for category, vendor, price range), sorting, infinite scroll, and no-results state.
acceptance_criteria: Search returns results within 100ms (Algolia SLA)
Facet filters update results without page reload
URL reflects active filters and search query (shareable links)
No-results state shows category suggestions
Search bar accessible: role=combobox, aria-expanded, keyboard navigation
Algolia search-only API key used client-side (never admin key)

plan.description: Build cart drawer and checkout flow: cart slide-over with line item CRUD, discount code input, order summary, multi-step checkout (address → shipping → payment with Stripe Elements), and order confirmation page.
acceptance_criteria: Cart drawer opens within 150ms of add-to-cart action
Line item quantity update debounced 300ms before API call
Stripe Elements loads with correct publishable key from env
Address form validates required fields client-side before submission
Successful payment redirects to /checkout/confirmation with order ID
Back button from checkout restores cart state
Payment form never logs card details to console or analytics

plan.description: Implement SEO layer: dynamic metadata (generateMetadata), Open Graph tags, JSON-LD structured data for Product and BreadcrumbList, XML sitemap generation, and robots.txt.
acceptance_criteria: Each PDP generates unique title tag and meta description from product data
Open Graph image uses product primary image with 1200x630 crop
JSON-LD Product schema validates in Google Rich Results Test
Sitemap.xml generated at build time with all product and collection URLs
robots.txt allows Googlebot and disallows /checkout/, /api/
No duplicate canonical tags on paginated PLP

plan.description: Produce PCI DSS SAQ-A compliance artifacts: scoping document confirming cardholder data handled solely by Stripe/Shopify, network segmentation notes, annual compliance attestation template, and evidence checklist.

payload.scope_justification: No cardholder data stored, processed, or transmitted by headless_storefront; delegated to Stripe

acceptance_criteria: SAQ-A questionnaire completed with pass/N/A for all 22 requirements
Scope reduction document signed off: confirms Stripe as payment processor, no PAN storage
Stripe webhook signature verification documented as compensating control
Annual review schedule defined
HTTPS-only enforcement documented (HSTS header, redirect from HTTP)

plan.description: Set up CI/CD pipeline (GitHub Actions): lint, type-check, unit tests, E2E tests (Playwright), Vercel preview deployments on PR, production deployment on main merge. Add Dependabot for dependency updates.
acceptance_criteria: CI pipeline completes in under 8 minutes on PR
Preview URL posted as PR comment with Vercel deployment link
Production deployment gated on all CI checks passing
Secrets (STRIPE_SECRET_KEY, ALGOLIA_ADMIN_KEY) stored in GitHub Secrets, not in code
Dependabot configured for weekly dependency updates on both npm and Docker
Rollback procedure documented: revert commit triggers automatic Vercel rollback

plan.description: Define infrastructure: Vercel project config (rewrites, headers for CSP/HSTS/cache), Medusa backend Dockerfile, PostgreSQL on managed service (Railway/Supabase), Redis for Medusa cache, environment variable management.
acceptance_criteria: Dockerfile builds Medusa backend image under 500MB
docker-compose.yml runs full local stack: web + backend + postgres + redis
vercel.json defines security headers: CSP, HSTS, X-Content-Type-Options
Cache-Control headers set: product pages 60s stale-while-revalidate, cart/checkout no-cache
All secrets injected via environment variables — no hardcoded credentials
Infrastructure cost estimate matches financial model (step 2)

plan.description: Write unit and integration tests: Medusa service unit tests (Jest), cart state Zustand store tests, API route tests with supertest, and Algolia sync subscriber tests with mocked Algolia client.
acceptance_criteria: CartService unit tests cover: create, addItem, updateQuantity, removeItem, applyDiscount
Algolia sync subscriber tests verify indexing called on product.created event
Stripe webhook handler tests cover succeeded and failed payment scenarios
80%+ line coverage achieved on all service files
Tests run in under 60 seconds in CI
No test relies on real external API calls (all mocked)

plan.description: Create QA test plan: functional test cases for all user journeys, cross-browser matrix (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, mobile), accessibility validation (axe-core), and performance budget checklist.
acceptance_criteria: Test plan covers all 5 user journeys with step-by-step test cases
Browser matrix executed with pass/fail for each journey per browser
axe-core scan returns 0 critical violations on PLP, PDP, cart, checkout
Lighthouse CI scores: Performance > 90, Accessibility > 95, SEO > 95 on PDP
Mobile viewport tested at 375px (iPhone SE) and 390px (iPhone 14)

plan.description: Write Playwright E2E test suite: full purchase flow (browse → search → PDP → cart → checkout → confirmation), cart persistence across page reload, search facet filtering, and Stripe test card scenarios.
acceptance_criteria: Full purchase flow test completes without manual intervention using Stripe test mode
Cart persists across browser refresh (localStorage hydration verified)
Search filter test confirms results narrow correctly with each facet
Declined card test verifies error message displayed and cart not cleared
All E2E tests pass in CI against preview deployment
E2E suite completes in under 5 minutes

plan.description: Define operational runbooks: deployment procedure, rollback steps, Algolia reindex runbook, database backup/restore, incident response for checkout outage, and SLA definitions (99.9% uptime, <2s TTFB).
acceptance_criteria: Deployment runbook includes pre-deploy checklist and smoke test steps
Rollback runbook executable in under 5 minutes
Algolia reindex runbook covers full catalog reindex without downtime
SLA document defines measurement methodology and exclusions
Incident response runbook assigns on-call roles and escalation path
Monitoring alerts configured for: error rate > 1%, p95 latency > 3s, payment failure spike

plan.description: Write developer documentation: local setup guide, environment variable reference, architecture decision records (ADRs) for Medusa vs Shopify and Algolia choice, API reference, and solution README.
acceptance_criteria: Local setup guide verified: new developer can run full stack from scratch following only README
All environment variables documented with type, required/optional, and example values
Three ADRs written with context, decision, and consequences sections
API reference covers all Medusa endpoints used by frontend
Architecture diagram (Mermaid) shows data flow: Browser → Next.js → Medusa/Shopify → Algolia → Stripe

flaws: Backend decision (Shopify vs Medusa) is never forced — Step 9 says 'if using Shopify only, skip migration setup', meaning the entire database schema, API design, and subscriber architecture could be thrown away mid-build. This is not a design choice, it's deferred technical debt disguised as flexibility.

Step 7 outputs both api_schema.graphql and openapi.yaml, but Medusa v2 exposes REST only — there is no GraphQL layer. If Shopify is chosen, the REST OpenAPI spec is irrelevant. The API contract deliverable is contradictory and will produce dead artifacts regardless of which backend is picked.

Cart state is split between Zustand/localStorage (client) and Medusa CartService (server). The plan has no strategy for guest-to-authenticated cart merge, concurrent-tab cart conflicts, or Medusa cart expiry (default 7 days) vs localStorage persistence. This is a known ecommerce production bug class.

ISR with revalidate: 60 on PDP means a product that sells out stays purchasable for up to 60 seconds post-depletion. No on-demand revalidation (Next.js revalidateTag/revalidatePath) is wired to the Medusa product update subscriber. The plan will ship a known oversell race condition.

PSD2/SCA Strong Customer Authentication is completely absent. Any EU customer paying with a European-issued card will trigger 3DS authentication. Stripe Elements handles the UX but the checkout flow (Step 16) must explicitly handle the PaymentIntent status requires_action and redirect the user back. Without this, EU checkout fails for a significant card percentage.

Step 4 (Legal) has no dependency on Step 6 (PRD) — legal docs are written before the product scope is defined. The privacy policy will document data collection that doesn't exist yet or miss collection that is later added. Legal depends on at minimum knowing what data flows exist.

PCI SAQ-A compliance work (Step 18) is scheduled after the payment implementation (Step 11). The scope definition — specifically whether any cardholder data touches the Next.js server during SSR — must be validated before writing checkout code, not after.

No email infrastructure is defined anywhere. Step 11 says 'order confirmation email triggered on payment_intent.succeeded' but there is no SMTP/transactional email provider setup (SendGrid, Postmark, Resend), no email template system, and no delivery failure handling. This is a dangling dependency with no owner step.

Tax calculation is entirely absent. Collecting payment without computing applicable sales tax (US nexus rules) or VAT (EU) is a legal compliance failure in most jurisdictions. No TaxJar, Avalara, or Stripe Tax integration is mentioned anywhere in 25 steps.

Inventory concurrency is unaddressed. Two simultaneous add-to-cart requests for the last unit will both succeed until checkout. No pessimistic locking, optimistic concurrency check, or reservation pattern is described. Medusa does not handle this automatically.
suggestions: Force the Shopify vs Medusa decision in Step 1 or as a prerequisite gate before Step 7. Document it as ADR-001 (already planned) and make Steps 9-12 conditional branches, not ambiguous prose. All downstream steps should reference the decided backend only.

Replace the dual GraphQL/REST API output in Step 7 with a single contract matching the chosen backend. If Shopify: document the Storefront GraphQL schema. If Medusa: document the REST OpenAPI spec. Delete the unused artifact.

Add a cart reconciliation spec to Step 10: define the merge strategy when a guest cart (Medusa cart_id in cookie) is associated with a newly authenticated customer. Medusa supports this via POST /store/customers/me/cart but it must be explicitly called.

Wire on-demand revalidation: in the Medusa product.updated subscriber (Step 12), call Next.js revalidateTag('product-[handle]') via a server action or internal API route. This eliminates the oversell window without removing ISR benefits.

Add a PSD2/SCA handling section to Step 16: after confirmPayment(), check the PaymentIntent status. If requires_action, call stripe.handleNextAction() or handleCardAction() and handle the return_url flow. Test with Stripe's 3DS test cards (4000002500003155).

Reorder Step 4 to depend on Step 6 (PRD). Legal docs need a defined feature scope to accurately describe data collection, retention, and third-party sharing. Running legal in parallel with market research produces legally incomplete documents.

Move PCI scope definition (currently Step 18) to before Step 11 implementation. The critical check — does any Next.js Server Component or API route ever receive or log raw card data — must be verified in the architecture phase, not after shipping.

Add Step 10.5 or extend Step 8: set up transactional email provider (Resend recommended for Medusa v2 compatibility), define email templates for order confirmation, shipping notification, and password reset, and add SMTP env vars to .env.example.

Add tax calculation to Step 10 backend implementation: integrate Stripe Tax (simplest path — one API flag on PaymentIntent) or Medusa's built-in tax module with region-based rates. Document jurisdiction coverage in the PRD.
missing: Inventory reservation / stock locking strategy during checkout (prevent oversell)
Tax computation provider and rate configuration
Transactional email infrastructure and template system
PSD2/SCA 3DS authentication handling in checkout flow
Password reset and account management flows (only register/login is specified)
Algolia index aliasing for zero-downtime reindexing (bulk reindex in Step 12 requires alias swap to avoid serving empty index during migration)
Image storage backend for Medusa (local disk fails in Docker/Railway — needs S3 or Cloudflare R2 configuration)
Error monitoring integration point (Sentry is mentioned in Step 24 ops runbook but never initialized in the application — Step 8 or 13 should install and configure it)
Rate limiting on Next.js API routes and Server Actions (Step 10 adds rate limiting to Medusa but the Next.js layer has none)
Webhook idempotency handling: Stripe can deliver the same webhook event multiple times; the payment_intent.succeeded handler must be idempotent using the event ID as a deduplication key
Medusa v2 plugin compatibility matrix — medusa-payment-stripe package name and API for v2 differs from v1; this needs explicit verification before Step 11
Shopify Storefront API access token scoping and rotating strategy (if Shopify path is chosen)
Content Security Policy for Stripe Elements: Stripe.js must be loaded from js.stripe.com and the CSP in Step 20 must whitelist exactly the right Stripe domains or payment will fail in production
security_risks: XSS via product content: Medusa product descriptions are merchant-controlled strings rendered in React. If any component uses dangerouslySetInnerHTML for rich text descriptions (common pattern), a compromised merchant account or admin injection attack yields stored XSS in a PCI-scoped checkout page. The threat model (Step 5) lists XSS but the frontend implementation steps have no explicit sanitization requirement.

ALGOLIA_ADMIN_KEY exposure: Step 19 correctly stores this in GitHub Secrets, but if any Next.js Server Component or API route forwards this key to the client response (e.g., in a debug header or error message), it grants full index write access. The search-only key restriction is mentioned but there is no audit requirement to verify the admin key never appears in client-accessible code paths.

Stripe webhook endpoint lacks idempotency: a replayed payment_intent.succeeded webhook will create a duplicate order. Without event ID deduplication in the database, this is both a financial and inventory integrity risk.

JWT refresh token strategy undefined: Step 10 specifies '1h expiry and refresh token' but the refresh token rotation policy, storage mechanism (httpOnly cookie vs localStorage), and revocation strategy are unspecified. Storing refresh tokens in localStorage makes them accessible to any XSS payload.

SSR secret leakage: Next.js App Router Server Components run server-side but developers frequently make the mistake of importing server-only modules into shared components, which Next.js then bundles client-side. Without the server-only package enforced at build time (mentioned nowhere), Medusa API keys or database credentials can leak into the client bundle.

CSP misconfiguration for Stripe: Stripe Elements requires specific CSP directives (frame-src https://js.stripe.com, script-src https://js.stripe.com). An overly strict or incorrectly specified CSP (Step 20) will silently break payment in some browsers with no obvious error, which is both a UX failure and a security misconfiguration if the fix is to loosen CSP globally.
final_review.summary: This is a structurally comprehensive plan that covers the right surface area for a headless ecommerce MVP, but it contains a critical unresolved fork — the Shopify vs Medusa backend decision — that contaminates every downstream step with ambiguity. Approximately 8 of 25 steps produce artifacts that are partially or wholly wrong depending on which backend is ultimately chosen, and no step forces the decision. Beyond this architectural defect, the plan has three production-failure-class gaps: cart split-brain between client and server with no reconciliation strategy, no PSD2/SCA 3DS handling that will cause silent checkout failures for EU users, and no tax computation layer that creates legal compliance exposure from day one. The security work is better than average (STRIDE model, PCI SAQ-A, SBOM) but is scheduled in the wrong order — PCI scope must precede payment implementation, not follow it. The score reflects a plan that would produce a demo-quality build that fails real users in checkout and fails regulators in EU markets without additional rework passes on these specific gaps.

Step 11 says 'order confirmation email triggered on payment_intent.succeeded' but there is no SMTP/transactional email provider setup (SendGrid, Postmark, Resend), no email template system, and no delivery failure handling. history.summary: This is a structurally comprehensive plan that covers the right surface area for a headless ecommerce MVP, but it contains a critical unresolved fork — the Shopify vs Medusa backend decision — that contaminates every downstream step with ambiguity. Approximately 8 of 25 steps produce artifacts that are partially or wholly wrong depending on which backend is ultimately chosen, and no step forces the decision. Beyond this architectural defect, the plan has three production-failure-class gaps: cart split-brain between client and server with no reconciliation strategy, no PSD2/SCA 3DS handling that will cause silent checkout failures for EU users, and no tax computation layer that creates legal compliance exposure from day one. The security work is better than average (STRIDE model, PCI SAQ-A, SBOM) but is scheduled in the wrong order — PCI scope must precede payment implementation, not follow it. The score reflects a plan that would produce a demo-quality build that fails real users in checkout and fails regulators in EU markets without additional rework passes on these specific gaps.
